content:
# Genesis Chapter 10 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 6r-6v | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_010_page_001.jpg`, `GEN_010_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 7-8 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**1** Now these are the generations of the sons of Noah: Shem, Ham, and Japheth. And sons were born to them after the flood.

**2** The sons of Japheth: Gomer, Magog, Madai, Javan, Tubal, Meshech, and Tiras.

**3** And the sons of Gomer: Ashkenaz, Riphath, and Togarmah.

**4** And the sons of Javan: Elishah, Tarshish, Kittim, and Dodanim.

**5** From these the coastland peoples spread out into their lands, each according to his language, according to their clans, in their nations.

**6** The sons of Ham: Cush, Egypt, Put, and Canaan.

**7** And the sons of Cush: Seba, Havilah, Sabtah, Raamah, and Sabteca. And the sons of Raamah: Sheba and Dedan.

**8** And Cush fathered Nimrod. He was the first to become a mighty man on the earth.

**9** He was a mighty hunter before the LORD; therefore it is said, "Like Nimrod, a mighty hunter before the LORD."

**10** And the beginning of his kingdom was Babel, Erech, Accad, and Calneh, in the land of Shinar.

**11** From that land he went out into Assyria and built Nineveh, Rehoboth-Ir, Calah,

**12** and Resen between Nineveh and Calah — that is the great city.

**13** Egypt fathered Ludim, Anamim, Lehabim, Naphtuhim,

**14** Pathrusim, Casluhim — from whom the Philistines came — and Caphtorim.

**15** Canaan fathered Sidon his firstborn, and Heth,

**16** and the Jebusite, the Amorite, the Girgashite,

**17** the Hivite, the Arkite, the Sinite,

**18** the Arvadite, the Zemarite, and the Hamathite. Afterward the clans of the Canaanites spread abroad.

**19** And the border of the Canaanites extended from Sidon in the direction of Gerar as far as Gaza, and in the direction of Sodom, Gomorrah, Admah, and Zeboiim, as far as Lasha.

**20** These are the sons of Ham, according to their clans, according to their languages, in their lands, in their nations.

**21** To Shem also, the father of all the children of Eber, the elder brother of Japheth, sons were born.

**22** The sons of Shem: Elam, Asshur, Arphaxad, Lud, and Aram.

**23** And the sons of Aram: Uz, Hul, Gether, and Mash.

**24** Arphaxad fathered Shelah, and Shelah fathered Eber.

**25** To Eber were born two sons: the name of the one was Peleg, for in his days the earth was divided; and his brother's name was Joktan.

**26** Joktan fathered Almodad, Sheleph, Hazarmaveth, Jerah,

**27** Hadoram, Uzal, Diklah,

**28** Obal, Abimael, Sheba,

**29** Ophir, Havilah, and Jobab. All these were the sons of Joktan.

**30** The territory where they settled extended from Mesha in the direction of Sephar, the hill country of the east.

**31** These are the sons of Shem, according to their clans, according to their languages, in their lands, according to their nations.

**32** These are the clans of the sons of Noah, according to their genealogies, in their nations. And from these the nations spread out over the earth after the flood.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 10 spans Folios 6r–6v.

   **Folio 6r** (GEN_010_page_001.jpg): This folio opens with the closing portion of Genesis 9 in its upper section — the death notice of Noah (9:28–29) is visible in the right-hand column at the top, marked with the familiar parashah structure of the Leningrad Codex. The toledot formula that opens Genesis 10 — אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת בְּנֵי נֹחַ (elleh toledot benei Noach, "these are the generations of the sons of Noah") — is legible at the transition point, with the standard three-column Masoretic layout. The text continues with the Japheth genealogy (vv. 2–5) and then the Ham genealogy (vv. 6–18), the Nimrod section (vv. 8–12) being particularly prominent in the center of the folio. The divine name יְהוָה (YHWH) is clearly visible in verses 9 (twice), corresponding to "before the LORD" in the Nimrod passage. The cantillation marks (te'amim) and vowel points (niqqud) are consistent throughout, confirming the standard Masoretic text. The folio carries the Ham genealogy through approximately verse 18, at which point the boundary section (vv. 19–20) begins.

   **Folio 6v** (GEN_010_page_002.jpg): This folio continues with the closing of the Ham section (vv. 19–20) and then the full Shem genealogy (vv. 21–31) and the concluding summary verse (v. 32). The Shem introduction in verse 21 is legible with its complex syntax: לְשֵׁם יֻלַּד גַּם הוּא (l'Shem yullad gam hu, "To Shem also...sons were born"). The name פֶּלֶג (Peleg) and the wordplay clause כִּי בְיָמָיו נִפְלְגָה הָאָרֶץ ("for in his days the earth was divided") are visible in verse 25. The folio continues with the Joktan sub-genealogy (vv. 26–29) and the territorial note (v. 30), closing with the summary formula of verses 31–32. The beginning of Genesis 11 is visible at the foot of this folio, confirming the textual continuity.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. The heading "CAP. X" is visible in the left margin of page 7 (GEN_010_vaticanus_page_001.jpg), marking the opening of Genesis 10 in the Vaticanus. The characteristic three-column uncial script is clear throughout the folio. The chapter heading "CAP. XI" appears at the bottom left of the same page, confirming that Genesis 10 ends and Genesis 11 begins within this single folio. Page 8 (GEN_010_vaticanus_page_002.jpg) carries Genesis 11 forward and then into Genesis 12 (CAP. XII visible at the top left), placing Genesis 10 entirely on page 7 of the Vaticanus. The Cainan variant (LXX v. 24) and other LXX divergences noted below were read from the uncial text of page 7.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת בְּנֵי נֹחַ — "Now these are the generations of the sons of Noah" (v. 1) — The Toledot Formula**

Genesis 10 opens with the fourth occurrence of the toledot formula (אֵלֶּה תּוֹלְדֹת, elleh toledot) in Genesis. The word תּוֹלְדֹת (toledot) is consistently rendered "generations" in the LOGOS formal translation — it is the technical Masoretic structural term for the genealogical-narrative sections that give Genesis its backbone, and the consistent English rendering "generations" preserves the reader's ability to track the structural device across the book (Gen 2:4; 5:1; 6:9; 10:1; 11:10; etc.).

The opening particle "now" (waw-consecutive) signals continuity from the preceding narrative: Genesis 9 ended with Noah's death; Genesis 10 now unfolds the proliferation of the world's peoples from his three sons. The formula covers all three sons — Shem, Ham, and Japheth — listed here in the order Shem-Ham-Japheth, though the chapter reverses the order of treatment: Japheth first (vv. 2–5), Ham second (vv. 6–20), Shem last (vv. 21–31). This literary reversal (treating Shem last, as the most significant line) is the standard Semitic technique of ending on the primary heir — Shem's line will carry the narrative toward Abraham.

**2. בְּנֵי יֶפֶת גֹּמֶר — The Sons of Japheth (vv. 2–5) — Geographical Identifications**

The Japhethite table names fourteen peoples/territories associated with the Indo-European and northern world from Israel's perspective. Several identifications are well-established in scholarship:

- **Gomer** (גֹּמֶר): Cimmerians, known from Assyrian records as Gimirri; associated with peoples north of the Black Sea and later in Anatolia.
- **Magog** (מָגוֹג): Uncertain; ancient sources variously identify with Scythians, Lydians, or northern peoples generally. In Ezekiel 38–39 Magog is the land of Gog. The LXX renders Μαγωγ (Magog) without interpretive gloss.
- **Madai** (מָדַי): Media, the Medes, attested in Assyrian records as Madai; a major Iranian people east of Mesopotamia.
- **Javan** (יָוָן, Yavan): Greece, the Ionians — Yavan/Iawan is the Hebrew/Semitic name for the Greeks from very early, and the identification is certain. The Vaticanus renders Ἰαυαν (Iavan) in close transliteration.
- **Tubal** (תּוּבַל): Associated with Tabali in Assyrian sources; southeastern Anatolia (later Cilicia). In Ezekiel paired repeatedly with Meshech.
- **Meshech** (מֶשֶׁךְ): Mushki in Assyrian records, associated with peoples of central Anatolia/Phrygia.
- **Tiras** (תִּירָס): Often identified with the Etruscans or Thracians; the identification is uncertain.

The formal translation renders all names in their standard English forms, following the conventions of major English Bible translations (ESV, KJV, NASB), rather than attempting to insert the modern geographical equivalents in the text itself. The identifications are noted here rather than inserted into the translation in order to maintain formal equivalence to the source.

The summary formula of verse 5 — "from these the coastland peoples spread out" — uses the Hebrew אִיִּים (iyyim, "coastlands, islands"), a term for the maritime nations of the Mediterranean world, especially the islands and coastal regions accessible by sea. The "coastlands" are the Japhethite peoples' characteristic dwelling — the sea-coasts and islands that characterize the Aegean, Mediterranean, and Black Sea regions. The formal translation renders "coastland peoples."

**3. כּוּשׁ יָלַד אֶת נִמְרֹד — "Cush fathered Nimrod" (v. 8) — The Nimrod Crux**

Verses 8–12 constitute the most narratively rich passage in the Table of Nations — a genealogical chapter that is otherwise a list of names suddenly breaks into extended prose narrative to describe Nimrod. This alone signals the figure's exceptional importance.

The name נִמְרֹד (Nimrod) does not appear elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible except in Micah 5:5, where "the land of Nimrod" is a poetic parallel for Assyria — suggesting that Nimrod was understood as the founding figure of the Assyrian/Mesopotamian world. The etymology of the name is disputed: some connect it to Hebrew מָרַד (marad, "to rebel"), reading "Nimrod" as "let us rebel" — making Nimrod an emblem of human defiance of God. Others see a Mesopotamian origin. The text itself does not gloss the name.

The phrase הוּא הֵחֵל לִהְיוֹת גִּבֹּר בָּאָרֶץ ("he was the first to become a mighty man on the earth") uses the verb חָלַל (chalal, Hiphil: הֵחֵל, "to begin, to be the first to do"). Nimrod is the "first" or "beginning" of something — not merely the first person to be called gibbor (mighty), but the originator of a kind of human greatness defined by power over the earth. The word גִּבּוֹר (gibbor, "mighty man, warrior, hero") is the same word used in Genesis 6:4 for the Nephilim — "the mighty men (gibborim) who were of old, men of renown." Nimrod's description connects him to that pre-flood tradition of superhuman power.

**4. גִּבֹּר צַיִד לִפְנֵי יְהוָה — "A mighty hunter before the LORD" (v. 9)**

This phrase is among the most discussed in the Table of Nations. Three words require careful attention:

- **גִּבֹּר** (gibbor): "mighty man, warrior, champion" — here applied to Nimrod's hunting. The formal translation renders "mighty hunter."
- **צַיִד** (tsayid): "hunt, game, hunting" — the word can refer both to the activity of hunting and to the prey itself. In context it means "hunter."
- **לִפְנֵי יְהוָה** (liphnei YHWH): "before the LORD, in the presence of the LORD, in the face of the LORD" — the prepositional phrase is the crux.

The phrase לִפְנֵי יְהוָה can mean:
1. **In the sight of / according to the judgment of the LORD** — a neutral phrase indicating that Nimrod's greatness was recognized by God Himself (compare "before God" in Genesis 6:11 where "the earth was corrupt before God" is an objective statement about God's perspective).
2. **Against the LORD / in defiance of the LORD** — a reading supported by some ancient interpreters who understood Nimrod as a figure of rebellion: he is mighty in the hunt *against* God's order.
3. **In the name of / with the blessing of the LORD** — Nimrod's prowess is celebrated in YHWH's name.

The ancient proverbial citation in the same verse — "therefore it is said, 'Like Nimrod, a mighty hunter before the LORD'" — indicates that the phrase became a cultural idiom for superlative greatness in hunting/warfare. The text presents the proverb as originating from Nimrod's reputation, not as a theological verdict. The formal translation renders the phrase literally: "before the LORD" — preserving the ambiguity of the Hebrew rather than resolving it in either a positive or negative direction.

**5. רֵאשִׁית מַמְלַכְתּוֹ בָּבֶל — "The beginning of his kingdom was Babel" (v. 10)**

The word רֵאשִׁית (reshit, "beginning, first, chief") links Nimrod's kingdom to earlier uses of the root in Genesis: Genesis 1:1 begins "in the beginning" (בְּרֵאשִׁית, bereshit), and Genesis 10:10 now speaks of the "beginning" of the first kingdom on earth. The connection may be intentional — Nimrod's kingdom is a counter-creation, a human beginning that echoes and competes with the divine beginning.

The cities of Nimrod's first kingdom are:
- **Babel** (בָּבֶל, Babel): The same Babel that will be named in Genesis 11:9 — Babylon. The connection between Nimrod's founding of Babel and the Tower of Babel narrative in Genesis 11 is textually significant: the table of nations locates the origin of Babel in Nimrod's ambition.
- **Erech** (אֶרֶךְ, Erech): Ancient Uruk, one of the earliest and greatest Sumerian cities (modern Warka in southern Iraq).
- **Accad** (אַכַּד, Akkad): The city and region that gave its name to the Akkadian language and empire (Sargon of Akkad's capital, c. 2300 BCE).
- **Calneh** (כַלְנֵה, Calneh): Less certain; ancient Nippur has been proposed, as has a north Syrian site. The LXX renders it Χαλαννη (Chalanne).

The phrase "in the land of Shinar" (בְּאֶרֶץ שִׁנְעָר, be'erets Shin'ar) identifies southern Mesopotamia — the same Shinar where the Tower of Babel is built in Genesis 11:2. The formal translation renders "Shinar" as the standard English form of the Hebrew name.

**6. מִן הָאָרֶץ הַהִוא יָצָא אַשּׁוּר — "From that land he went out into Assyria" (v. 11)**

The subject of "he went out" (יָצָא, yatsa) is disputed. The Hebrew is ambiguous: it could be (a) Nimrod himself — he expanded his empire northward into Assyria; or (b) Asshur (Assyria personified) — he went out from that land.

The formal translation reads Nimrod as the subject for two reasons: (1) the preceding verses (8–10) have Nimrod as the consistent grammatical subject, and the next clause continues the account of city-founding that has characterized Nimrod's activity; (2) the verb form (third-person masculine singular) has Nimrod as its most natural antecedent. This reading is supported by the Targum Onkelos, which explicitly names Nimrod as the subject here: Nimrod went forth from Shinar and built Nineveh.

The alternative reading (Asshur as subject) is supported by some ancient interpreters and modern scholars on the grounds that Asshur appears among the sons of Shem (v. 22), not Ham, and that Asshur as the eponymous ancestor of Assyria is the natural builder of Nineveh. However, the MT's text places this verse within the Nimrod narrative and after the Hamite genealogy — the formal translation follows the grammatical continuity and renders Nimrod as the one who went out.

**7. לְשֵׁם יֻלַּד גַּם הוּא — "To Shem also... sons were born" (v. 21) — The Shem Introduction**

The introduction of Shem's genealogy is the most syntactically unusual verse in the chapter. The Hebrew reads:

לְשֵׁם יֻלַּד גַּם הוּא אֲבִי כָּל בְּנֵי עֵבֶר אֲחִי יֶפֶת הַגָּדוֹל

Literally: "To Shem was born also he, father of all the sons of Eber, brother of Japheth the great."

The syntax presents two interpretive challenges:

**Challenge 1:** The adjective הַגָּדוֹל (ha-gadol, "the great") — does it modify Japheth ("Japheth the great") or Shem ("the elder brother of Japheth")?

If it modifies Japheth: Shem is the brother of Japheth-the-great (i.e., the firstborn/elder). But Japheth is listed first among the sons in verse 1 and is often taken as the eldest.

If it modifies Shem via the relationship: Shem is "the elder brother" (with "the great" indicating seniority) of Japheth. This reading makes Shem the elder — a position supported by the Septuagint, which renders "Shem, the elder brother of Japheth" (Σημ... ὁ ἀδελφὸς Ιαφεθ τοῦ μείζονος).

The formal translation renders "the elder brother of Japheth" — reading הַגָּדוֹל as characterizing Shem's seniority over Japheth. This follows the LXX reading and is grammatically defensible (הַגָּדוֹל agrees with אֲחִי, "brother," making Shem the greater/elder brother).

**Challenge 2:** The phrase אֲבִי כָּל בְּנֵי עֵבֶר ("father of all the children of Eber") — why is Shem specifically identified this way?

The designation "father of all the children of Eber" (avei kol benei Eber) is a link between Shem and the line that will eventually reach Abraham. The name עֵבֶר (Eber) is the eponymous ancestor of the Hebrews (עִבְרִי, ivri, "Hebrew") — the word "Hebrew" derives from Eber's name. By identifying Shem as "the father of all the children of Eber," the text signals that Shem's most significant legacy is the line that produces the Hebrew people. This anticipatory identification is a structural signal that the genealogy about to be presented (Shem → Arphaxad → Shelah → Eber → Peleg → ...) leads to the most significant lineage in the entire Table of Nations.

**8. פֶּלֶג כִּי בְיָמָיו נִפְלְגָה הָאָרֶץ — "Peleg, for in his days the earth was divided" (v. 25)**

This is the most significant wordplay in Genesis 10. The name פֶּלֶג (Peleg) is explained by the verb נִפְלְגָה (niplega, Niphal of פָּלַג, palag, "to divide, to split"). The naming convention follows the standard biblical pattern: a child's name encodes the meaning of a historical event at the time of birth (compare Eve, Cain, Seth, Noah — all named with etymological wordplays in Genesis 2–5).

The "dividing of the earth" (נִפְלְגָה הָאָרֶץ) in Peleg's days is most naturally understood as a reference to the event of Genesis 11:1–9 — the scattering of the nations at the Tower of Babel, where God "divided" (confusing their language and dispersing the peoples). The same root פָּלַג does not appear in Genesis 11, but the concept of division/dispersal is identical, and the Table of Nations of Genesis 10 and the Babel narrative of Genesis 11 are closely linked: Genesis 10 describes the dispersed nations, Genesis 11 explains how the dispersion happened. The naming of Peleg anchors this event to a specific generation in the Shem genealogy.

The wordplay in English cannot be fully reproduced, but a footnote-worthy observation: the name "Peleg" and the English verb "to peel apart" or "to split" share no etymological connection. Some commentators note that the ancient Greek name Pelagios (sea) and the Hebrew palag (divide) may reflect a broader Semitic-Indo-European convergence on the idea of water-division, but this is speculative. The formal translation preserves the Hebrew wordplay by explicitly stating the etymological connection in the text ("for in his days the earth was divided") — the English reader can perceive the naming logic even if the phonetic echo is lost.

**9. אֵלֶּה מִשְׁפְּחֹת בְּנֵי נֹחַ — "These are the clans of the sons of Noah" (v. 32) — The Conclusion**

The closing verse of the chapter uses the word מִשְׁפָּחוֹת (mishpachot, "clans, families") rather than the earlier structural term "sons." The word mishpachah denotes the kinship group — the extended family, the clan — and is appropriate here because by verse 32 the text has moved from individual sons to organized kinship groups constituting nations. The summary is comprehensive: these are the clans of all Noah's sons, according to their genealogies (תּוֹלְדֹתָם, toledotam — the root toledot again), in their nations, and from these the nations spread over the earth after the flood.

The phrase "from these the nations spread out over the earth" (מֵאֵלֶּה נִפְרְדוּ הַגּוֹיִם בָּאָרֶץ אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל) uses the verb פָּרַד (parad, "to spread out, to separate, to divide") — a different verb from פָּלַג but in the same semantic range. The nations were not merely born; they spread out and separated — the Table of Nations is a record of differentiation, divergence, and dispersal, grounded in the single family of Noah.

The final phrase "after the flood" (אַחַר הַמַּבּוּל, achar ha-mabbul) mirrors the same phrase used in 10:1 and closes the chapter in an inclusio: the entire Table of Nations is bracketed by the flood as its precondition and its context.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 10

The Vaticanus manuscript image for Genesis 10 (page 7, GEN_010_vaticanus_page_001.jpg) was read directly. The chapter heading CAP. X is visible in the left margin. The following variants from the MT are noted:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 10:2 | דֹּדָנִים (Dodanim) | Ῥόδιοι (Rhodioi, "Rhodians") | "Dodanim" | MT reads Dodanim; 1 Chr 1:7 reads Rodanim — the LXX renders Rhodians, identifying with Rhodes; MT followed as primary |
| 10:4 | דֹּדָנִים (Dodanim) | Ῥόδιοι (Rhodioi) | "Dodanim" | Same as above |
| 10:10 | כַלְנֵה (Calneh) | Χαλαννη (Chalanne) | "Calneh" | Close transliteration; no significant divergence |
| 10:14 | וְכַסְלֻחִים אֲשֶׁר יָצְאוּ מִשָּׁם פְּלִשְׁתִּים (Casluhim, from whom came the Philistines) | LXX preserves "from whom came the Philistines" after Casluhim | "Casluhim — from whom the Philistines came" | The parenthetical in MT and LXX agree; the clause may be misplaced (Caphtorim, not Casluhim, is the source of the Philistines per Amos 9:7 and Deut 2:23); formal translation renders MT as it stands |
| 10:21 | אֲחִי יֶפֶת הַגָּדוֹל ("brother of Japheth the great/elder") | ὁ ἀδελφὸς Ιαφεθ τοῦ μείζονος ("the brother of the greater Japheth" or "the elder brother of Japheth") | "the elder brother of Japheth" | LXX τοῦ μείζονος ("the greater") may modify Japheth (making Japheth the greater) or may indicate Shem's seniority as elder; formal translation renders Shem as "elder brother" following LXX contextual sense |
| 10:24 | אַרְפַּכְשַׁד יָלַד אֶת שֶׁלַח (Arphaxad fathered Shelah) | LXX: Αρφαξαδ ἐγέννησεν τὸν Καϊναν, Καϊναν δὲ ἐγέννησεν τὸν Σαλα (Arphaxad fathered Cainan, and Cainan fathered Shelah) | "Arphaxad fathered Shelah" | **Major variant.** LXX inserts Cainan (Kainan) as an extra generation between Arphaxad and Shelah. This Cainan is absent from the MT entirely and is one of the most significant MT/LXX divergences in Genesis. Luke 3:36 follows the LXX by including Cainan in Jesus' genealogy. MT followed as primary; see extended note below |
| 10:28 | עוֹבָל (Obal) | Εβαλ (Ebal) | "Obal" | MT Obal / 1 Chr 1:22 also Ebal; LXX Ebal; MT Genesis reading followed |

**Extended Note — The Cainan Variant (v. 24):**

The insertion of a generation "Cainan" (Kainan/Qayin) between Arphaxad and Shelah in the LXX is the most theologically significant textual variant in Genesis 10. The MT reads: Arphaxad → Shelah → Eber → Peleg. The LXX reads: Arphaxad → Cainan → Shelah → Eber → Peleg.

This extra generation appears consistently in the LXX Genesis 10:24 and again in Genesis 11:12–13, where the LXX gives Cainan a lifespan (130 years before fathering Shelah, then 330 years more, total 460 years). The New Testament genealogy of Luke 3:36 includes "Cainan, son of Arphaxad" in Jesus' lineage — following the LXX against the MT.

The question of whether this Cainan is an original reading accidentally dropped from the MT, or a LXX expansion harmonizing with the earlier Cainan of Genesis 5:9–14 (a pre-flood patriarch), or a scribal insertion in the LXX tradition, has been debated extensively. The Dead Sea Scrolls do not provide a clear resolution for this specific verse. The formal LOGOS translation follows the MT as its primary source and does not include Cainan in verse 24; the LXX variant is documented here for the reader's awareness and for those who follow the LXX tradition (including Luke's genealogy).

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**The Table of Nations as Theological Architecture**

Genesis 10 — the Table of Nations — is unique in ancient literature. No other document from the ancient Near East presents a systematic genealogical account of the origin of all known peoples with this comprehensiveness or theological intentionality. Cuneiform king lists, Egyptian records of foreign peoples, and Mesopotamian cosmological texts all deal with particular nations or dynasties, but Genesis 10 attempts to map the entire known human world as a family descending from a single man.

The theological claim embedded in this genealogical form is radical: all nations are one family. The seventy nations listed in Genesis 10 (a count that varies slightly between MT and LXX) are all children of Noah, and through Noah, children of Adam and Eve. There is no nation that is not in the family of God's image-bearers. The Table of Nations is the universal scope of the Noahic covenant (Genesis 9:8–17) expressed in genealogical form — every nation listed here is a covenant partner in the covenant of the rainbow.

**The Three-Part Structure**

The chapter is organized into three genealogical sections, each closed with a summary formula:

- **Japheth** (vv. 2–5): "From these the coastland peoples spread out..." (v. 5)
- **Ham** (vv. 6–20): "These are the sons of Ham, according to their clans..." (v. 20)
- **Shem** (vv. 21–31): "These are the sons of Shem, according to their clans..." (v. 31)

The reversal of birth-order treatment (Japheth first, Shem last) is deliberate. The narrative purpose of the Table of Nations is to set the stage for Genesis 11:27 — the toledot of Terah, which begins the Abraham narrative. Shem is placed last because his line is the one the narrative will follow — ending the chapter with Shem's genealogy positions the reader to enter the Babel narrative of Genesis 11 and then the Abrahamic narrative of Genesis 12 with Shem's line fresh in mind.

**Nimrod and Empire**

The Nimrod passage (vv. 8–12) is the only place in the Table of Nations where genealogical listing gives way to extended narrative prose. The transition is significant: among all the seventy nations, only Nimrod receives a biography. And his biography is specifically about the founding of cities and kingdoms — about the emergence of organized political power, empire, and the centralization of human authority in the land of Shinar.

Read in the light of what follows in Genesis 11 (the Tower of Babel, built in Shinar, by people using brick and mortar to build a city and a tower), Nimrod's founding of Babel in Shinar takes on ominous resonance. The Table of Nations presents Nimrod's kingdom as the origin-point of the very civilization that Genesis 11 will judge. The phrase "a mighty hunter before the LORD" — whether read as praise, irony, or indictment — marks Nimrod as a figure of extraordinary human power whose legacy is the first kingdom on earth.

**"The Earth Was Divided" — Peleg and the Babel Connection**

The notation in verse 25 that "in his days the earth was divided" (נִפְלְגָה הָאָרֶץ) is the Table of Nations' internal cross-reference to the Babel event of Genesis 11. The Table of Nations and the Babel narrative are commonly understood by scholars as two complementary perspectives on the same historical reality — the dispersal of the nations. Genesis 10 describes the dispersal genealogically and geographically; Genesis 11:1–9 describes it causally (through language confusion and divine dispersion). The naming of Peleg anchors the dispersal to a specific human moment within Shem's lineage, connecting the international scope of Genesis 10 to the intimate genealogical account of Genesis 11.

**The Seventy Nations**

The MT text of Genesis 10 lists seventy individual names (by the traditional count), a number that achieves significance in the broader biblical symbolic system: seventy is ten times seven, combining the numbers of completeness. The descendants of Israel who go down to Egypt in Genesis 46 are also seventy. The seventy elders of Israel in Numbers 11 and the seventy nations of the Table of Nations frame Israel's existence between two seventies — the world's peoples and Israel's founding fathers — as a way of signaling that Israel is one nation among the family of all nations, neither alone nor supreme, but the particular people through whom the universal blessing of Abraham will reach all the seventy nations of the earth (Genesis 12:3: "in you all the families of the earth shall be blessed").
